claims.text: Dulce, a small town in northern New Mexico on the Jicarilla Apache reservation near the Colorado border, reportedly has the highest per-capita UFO sighting rate in the world and is alleged to sit above an underground alien base.

evidence.quote:
Dulce is a town in New Mexico near the
Colorado border on the hickorya Apache
reservation

claims.rationale: Dulce exists on the Jicarilla Apache reservation (the host's transcript says 'hickorya'). The cattle-mutilation anomaly around Dulce is historically real (Gabe Valdez, the APRO investigations). The 'highest per-capita UFO sightings' claim is a frequently-cited statistic without rigorous sourcing; the underground base claim has no physical evidence. Directional geographical facts are correct; the 'underground alien base' framing is unsupported.

claims.text: In 1975, Officer Gabe Valdez investigated a cow mutilation near Dulce where the animal had been surgically drained of blood, had organs removed, and left with tripod tracks and scorched ground — the first of ~8,000 animal mutilations later catalogued in the region over the following years.

evidence.quote:
in 1975 a rancher in Dulce
called the police in a panic one of his
cattle had been killed

claims.rationale: Gabe Valdez was a real New Mexico State Police officer who spent decades documenting cattle mutilations starting in the mid-1970s. The specific anomalies (surgical precision, drained blood, missing organs, tripod marks) are consistently reported across Valdez's and others' case files. The 8,000 figure is a regional estimate often cited by UFO researchers; mainstream explanations attribute most cases to scavenger action.

claims.text: The FBI's official investigation of the Dulce-area cattle mutilations concluded the deaths were consistent with ordinary predator action and that there was no federal interest in continuing the investigation — a conclusion the video presents derisively.

evidence.quote:
the FBI said quote it was
consistent with predator action and
there is no federal interest in
continuing an investigation

claims.rationale: The FBI did produce reports (the Rommel Report, 1980, and subsequent memos) concluding predator action — especially coyotes, skunks, and rodents post-mortem — explains the cattle deaths. Soft-tissue bloating and scavenger-preferred areas (eye, rectum, tongue) create the appearance of surgical removal. The FBI finding is well-documented; host's sarcastic framing ('the only predator that can do this is the Predator') is rhetorical pushback.

claims.text: At the 1995 Preparedness Expo, engineer Phil Schneider publicly claimed to have survived a 1979 firefight — the 'Battle of Dulce' — in which 66 US military personnel were killed after accidentally drilling into an underground alien base that had existed for 400-500 years.

evidence.quote:
66 men
lost their lives in what was called the
Battle of Dulce base the American
Military had accidentally drilled into
an alien base that had been there for
four or five hundred years

claims.rationale: Phil Schneider genuinely gave this talk at the 1995 Preparedness Expo; recordings circulate widely. The factual content — Battle of Dulce, 66 casualties, centuries-old alien base, plasma-weapon amputation — has no corroborating evidence, no casualty records, no missing-persons reports that match. The host later systematically debunks Schneider's specifics (NATO/Delta/Secret Service anachronisms). directTruth low for the substantive claim.

claims.text: Phil Schneider's 1979 Battle-of-Dulce narrative is internally inconsistent with US military history — Delta Force was barely 18 months old and classified in 1979, NATO does not operate on US soil, and the Secret Service is a law-enforcement agency that would not provide facility security.

evidence.quote:
in 1979 Delta Force was
barely 18 months old very few people
even knew about it

claims.rationale: [logical] Historically correct. 1st SFOD-D (Delta Force) was established November 1977 by Charlie Beckwith. NATO is a foreign-affairs collective defense alliance with no domestic US deployment authority (Posse Comitatus also applies). USSS protects officials; it is not a tactical security force. Strong internal-consistency disqualifier.

claims.text: Phil Schneider's father, Oscar Schneider, was not a captured Nazi U-boat captain — he was born in San Francisco in 1906 and served in the US Navy Medical Corps 1931-1946, retiring as Captain, a medical doctor specializing in shipboard safety.

evidence.quote:
father was actually born in San
Francisco in 1906.

claims.rationale: [debunks] Oscar Schneider's US naval service record and birth details are verifiable public record. Phil Schneider repeatedly told audiences his father captained a captured Nazi U-boat and later worked on the Philadelphia Experiment — falsified by Oscar's US birth certificate, continuous US presence, and medical (not combatant) naval MOS. Direct biographical debunk of Phil's self-presentation.

claims.text: Phil Schneider claimed that the US government has signed four treaties with aliens (1944, 1954, 1962, and 1979 after the Dulce battle) including the 'Greada' treaty allowing abductions and experimentation on humans.

evidence.quote:
S government
signed four treaties with the aliens in
1944 in 1954 1962 and the last one in
1979 after the Dolce battle

claims.rationale: Schneider did make these claims on the 1995 lecture circuit. Factually: Eisenhower's supposed Holloman AFB meeting with aliens (the 'Greada/Grenada Treaty') is a staple of UFO conspiracy folklore with zero documentary evidence in declassified archives. The specific four-date treaty list appears nowhere outside the UFO conspiracy literature.

claims.text: Phil Schneider claimed both the 1989 Loma Prieta (San Francisco) earthquake and the 1995 Kobe earthquake were US-military weapons tests using alien-provided seismic technology.

evidence.quote:
the 1989 San Francisco
earthquake and the 1995 Kobe earthquake
were weapons tests

claims.rationale: Schneider made the claim. Factually unsupported: the Loma Prieta (Mw 6.9, Oct 17, 1989) and Kobe/Great Hanshin (Mw 6.9, Jan 17, 1995) earthquakes are extensively modeled geophysically — both fit well within natural seismicity patterns on known fault systems (San Andreas; Nojima Fault). No seismic-weapon technology is known to science.

claims.text: Phil Schneider additionally claimed the 1993 World Trade Center bombing and the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing were carried out with miniature nuclear devices provided by aliens, and that AIDS was a population-control bioweapon.

evidence.quote:
he said the bombings of the
World Trade Center in 1993 and the
Oklahoma City bombing of 1995 were done
with miniature nuclear devices

claims.rationale: Factually absurd. The 1993 WTC bombing used a urea nitrate-hydrogen gas cylinder truck bomb (Ramzi Yousef convicted); the 1995 OKC bombing used an ANFO truck bomb by McVeigh and Nichols (both convicted, with detailed forensic evidence). AIDS is caused by HIV, a retrovirus with a well-mapped zoonotic origin. Schneider's claims combine several known-discredited conspiracy memes.

claims.text: Phil Schneider was found dead in his apartment in January 1996, four months after his famous Preparedness Expo lecture, with a medical tube wrapped three times around his neck — officially ruled suicide but with multiple anomalies (missing blood/urine samples, missing effects, fake obituary).

evidence.quote:
in January 1996 just four
months after he gave his famous lecture
Phil Schneider was found dead in his
apartment

claims.rationale: Schneider's death (January 17, 1996 in Wilsonville, Oregon) is documented. The circumstances were indeed unusual enough that the manner of death was initially undetermined; his ex-wife Cynthia Drayer has publicly disputed the suicide ruling. The anomalies the host lists (missing samples, strangulation configuration despite physical limitations) are corroborated by Drayer's public statements.

claims.text: Phil Schneider had a documented history of mental illness, and many of his claims (28% of US GDP in black-ops, specific treaty dates) resemble the richly-detailed narratives characteristic of paranoid delusion.

evidence.quote:
Phil Schneider had a documented
history of mental illness

claims.rationale: [undercuts] Publicly-documented fact. Schneider was reportedly under psychiatric treatment and had been diagnosed; his ex-wife has described his declining mental state. Does not by itself disprove his claims but explains the specific-number granularity (28%, 66 soldiers, 131 bases) characteristic of delusional systems. The host presents this as the most charitable skeptical interpretation.

claims.text: The entire Dulce-base mythology traces back to a single person — Thomas Levesque (aka James Bishop) — who published the newsletter 'Hollow Earth Hassle' in the 1970s and seeded the story through pulp-era science fiction motifs.

evidence.quote:
the Chief Architect of the
Dulce base story in the 70s Levesque

claims.rationale: Traced in Adam Gorightly's 'Saucers, Spooks and Kooks' (2021) which identifies Thomas Bowyn/Levesque as the originator. The newsletter 'Hollow Earth Hassle' and its circulation are documented. Shaver-mystery connections (Richard Shaver, Amazing Stories 1945-48) as source material are well-attested in UFO-history literature.

claims.text: No record of anyone named Thomas Castello — the other key Dulce-base whistleblower, supposedly a military photographer with top-secret clearance — has been found in US military rosters for the period he claimed to work there.

evidence.quote:
no
record of anyone named Thomas Castello
was found in the U.S military

claims.rationale: [debunks] Consistent with researchers' exhaustive checks. Castello's entire body of 'testimony' reaches the public through the 'Bratton interview' and 'Dulce papers' which are not accompanied by any verifiable Bratton identity, and Castello himself is not confirmed in Army/AF/Navy service records, civilian-contractor directories for Nellis-area operations, or residential records of the period.

claims.text: The iconic 'humans in pods' photograph attributed to Thomas Castello is actually a screenshot from the 2000 Arnold Schwarzenegger film The Sixth Day — unambiguous evidence of fabricated photographic evidence.

evidence.quote:
Thomas
Castello didn't take that picture it's
actually a screenshot of the film The
Sixth Day released in 2000.

claims.rationale: [debunks] Verifiable by image comparison — the purported 'leaked Dulce photograph' matches a frame from the 2000 film The Sixth Day. This is the most straightforward and damning piece of debunking in the episode. Independent researchers have published side-by-side comparisons.

claims.text: It is documented that US Air Force OSI agent Richard Doty ran an active misinformation campaign against businessman Paul Bennewitz in the 1980s, confirming Bennewitz's alien suspicions as part of a deliberate effort to mislead the UFO community.

evidence.quote:
agent Richard Doty of the
Air Force OSI perpetrated a
misinformation campaign against Paul
benowitz

claims.rationale: Well-documented. Doty has publicly acknowledged his role in the Bennewitz affair (see Greg Bishop's 'Project Beta', 2005, and Mark Pilkington's 'Mirage Men', 2010 book/film). Bennewitz was fed fake alien-intelligence material leading to his mental breakdown. The episode correctly cites one of the most credentialed pieces of 'the US government has deliberately disinformed UFO researchers' evidence.
